summary: Gruber dismantles a Gartner analyst report claiming Apple priced new iPods for profit margins rather than market share growth. He argues the analysts ignore supply constraints, the economics of maximizing total profit (not just margin), and the competitive landscape where Apple's prices match or undercut competitors like SanDisk. He points out that Apple's simplified product line is itself a strategic advantage, not a missed opportunity. The piece concludes that these analysts are operating from an outdated narrative that paints Apple customers as overpaying zealots, and that syndication of one flawed story creates a false impression of widespread concern.
key_points: When demand outstrips supply, pricing higher is rational economics — Apple may have left money on the table during previous holiday shortages by pricing too low
Margin is only one component of profit; the goal is to maximize the product of margin and unit sales, not either one in isolation
Apple's iPod prices are competitive with SanDisk's comparable products despite Apple offering a superior device, undermining the 'overpriced' narrative
The simplicity of Apple's product line and clean pricing tiers ($150/$200/$250) is itself a strategic brand asset, not a failure to segment the market
Analysts and reporters are recycling an outdated script where Apple products are dismissed as overpriced and their customers as cultists
money_quotes: Simple economics dictates that when demand outstrips supply, the price is too low.
The goal isn't to maximize margin or unit sales, the goal is to maximize the product of margin and unit sales — the profit.
The relative simplicity of their product lines is a key part of the Apple experience.
To the victor go the spoils; when you've got a smash hit and a strong brand on your hands, you can sell for higher margins and increase (or at least maintain) your market share.
And why should Apple's prices be judged by the cost of their component materials, anyway? Shouldn't they be judged against the rest of the market?
I suggest they head to a nearby high school and ask a few students whether an iPod Nano is easily worth $40 more than a SanDisk e250.

What I think is going on is that these analysts, along with tech reporters like Dan Nystedt, are still reading from the old script where Apple products get written off as overpriced and their customers branded as mindless cultists.
The idea is to paint Apple as a company that milks its own loyal customers. What other company gets held to a standard like this?
key_insight: Judging Apple's pricing by component cost rather than competitive positioning and total profit optimization reveals analysts still trapped in a narrative that dismisses Apple's brand power as customer exploitation.
